content: ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) investing integrates non-financial factors into investment decisions. Approaches include: negative screening (excluding sin stocks), ESG integration (weighting ESG scores in analysis), thematic investing (clean energy, water), and impact investing (measurable social/environmental outcomes). Assets under management in ESG funds exceeded $35 trillion globally in 2024. Debate centers on whether ESG sacrifices returns for values or identifies material risks/opportunities. Evidence is mixed: some studies show ESG portfolios have similar or slightly better risk-adjusted returns due to lower tail risk; others find underperformance from constrained universes. Greenwashing—exaggerating ESG credentials—is a concern. Regulatory frameworks (EU SFDR, SEC proposals) aim to standardize disclosures.
